questions.prompt: A modeller is searching for available data to evaluate aerosol particle size distributions in their model. Which of these instruments is least likely to be useful?
questions.hint: Think about the size range of the instruments.
questions.answer: The CDP (cloud droplet probe) is designed to measure cloud droplets, not aerosol particles, so its diameter range is typically too large to be useful. However, some scientists have used the CDP to measure coarse-mode aerosol by changing the assumptions made about the aerosol refractive index (Rosenberg, et al., 2012, AMT)
author: Ken Carslaw
Erin Raif
reference: Rosenberg, P. D., Dean, A. R., Williams, P. I., Dorsey, J. R., Minikin, A., Pickering, M. A., and Petzold, A.: Particle sizing calibration with refractive index correction for light scattering optical particle counters and impacts upon PCASP and CDP data collected during the Fennec campaign, Atmos. Meas. Tech., 5, 1147-1163, https://doi.org/10.5194/amt-5-1147-2012, 2012.

Raif, E. N., Barr, S. L., Tarn, M. D., McQuaid, J. B., Daily, M. I., Abel, S. J., Barrett, P. A., Bower, K. N., Field, P. R., Carslaw, K. S., and Murray, B. J.: High ice-nucleating particle concentrations associated with Arctic haze in springtime cold-air outbreaks, Atmos. Chem. Phys., 24, 14045–14072, https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-24-14045-2024, 2024.

questions.prompt: A modeller is searching for available data to evaluate the amount of soot in their model. Which instrument's data would be most useful?
questions.answer: The SP2 (Single Particle Soot Photometer) selectively vaporises black carbon.
author: Ken Carslaw

questions.prompt: Which combination of instruments would be most appropriate for measuring new particle formation
questions.hint: Consider the size range and capabilities of each instrument.
author: Ken Carslaw
Erin Raif

questions.prompt: An air sampler uses a PM2.5 filter. What proportion of aerosols with diameter 2.5 µm would you expect to pass through this PM2.5 filter?
questions.hint: Consider how the transmission rate changes as the diameter changes, and how this applies to the PM2.5 filter.
questions.answer: 50%. People commonly mistakenly think this is 0%.
author: Erin Raif

questions.prompt: A technique to measure INPs involves applying droplets of purified water to a filter which collects aerosols. Is this technique online or offline?
questions.answer: Offline, the analysis is performed after collection.
author: Erin Raif

questions.prompt: What is the time resolution of a filter measurement?
author: Erin Raif
options: The length of time each filter is exposed to aerosol
The time between different filter measurements
The time between the collection of the filter and the analysis of the filter

questions.prompt: A scientist is measuring coarse-mode aerosol using an instrument that uses a sampling line.
questions.answer: Different physical processes dominate particle losses depending on the particle size.
author: Erin Raif

parts.prompt: Which are the most likely sources of particle losses?
parts.label: (a)
parts.answer: Impaction and gravitational settling losses dominate when the particle diameter is large.

parts.prompt: What physical shape of line would mitigate these best?
parts.label: (b)

questions.prompt: An aerosol-climate model with a precise aerosol emission inventory has too few aerosols in the upper troposphere. What is a plausible reason for this?
author: Erin Raif
options: Aerosol mixes too quickly in a grid-box, so aerosols form droplets too rapidly leading to too-efficient precipitation.

questions.prompt: Suggest a list of instruments that could be used on an aircraft campaign to measure aerosol-cloud interactions.
author: Ken Carslaw

questions.prompt: Why might a single air inlet to measure aerosol utilise both a PM2.5 filter and a PM10 filter in series?
questions.answer: To avoid larger particles clogging the instrument.
author: Erin Raif

questions.prompt: An IAGOS aircraft measurement of a wildfire plume at the tropopause reports a black carbon concentration of 154 s cm-3.
questions.answer: Aerosol measurements cannot be understood independently from the atmospheric conditions they were made in!
author: Erin Raif

parts.prompt: Given that the measurement was made at 250 hPa and -19.0 °C, calculate the ambient concentration recorded by the instrument.
parts.label: (a)
parts.answerInfo: Using the ideal gas law, \(PV=nRT\), the ratio between the volume of ambient air and air at STP is \(T_{\mathrm{amb.}}P_{\mathrm{STP}}/T_{\mathrm{STP}}P_{\mathrm{amb.}}\). To convert between STP and ambient concentrations, divide by this ratio. Since this was IAGOS data, found on EDAS (Sec. 9.3.2), the value of STP is 1013 hPa. If you used the IUPAC value of 1000 hPa, you would have calculated 41.3 cm-3. That's probably close enough for our purposes, but it could lead to confusion in some cases!

parts.prompt: When using these measurements to set a vertical profile of aerosol concentration, a modeller fails to spot that the concentration is in s cm-3, instead thinking it is in cm-3. What would be the consequence of this error?
parts.label: (b)
parts.answer: The aerosol concentration would be increasingly overestimated with altitude as the air density decreases.

questions.prompt: A scientist wishes to obtain a particle-size distribution of sodium chloride aerosol via an unheated inlet using an online technique. Aside from the ambient temperature and pressure, which atmospheric quantity should they measure?
questions.answer: Relative humidity. Sodium chloride is a strongly hygroscopic aerosol and will swell at high humidities. This can be corrected for using the hygroscopic growth factor, but the relative humidity must be known.
author: Erin Raif
